does: This paper introduces Item Response Scaling Laws (IRSL), a framework that combines Item Response Theory with scaling law estimation to predict language model performance efficiently. The core contribution is factorizing the scaling relationship between models and questions from O(M×N) parameters down to O(M+N), dramatically reducing computational requirements. The authors instantiate this with Beta-IRT, which uses empirical probability responses like token probabilities and pass rates rather than binary correctness labels. They validate IRSL across pre-training downstream scaling using 6,612 LM checkpoints and 37,682 questions from 10 benchmarks, demonstrating that the approach can estimate scaling laws while requiring far fewer checkpoint evaluations than traditional methods.
connects: This work builds on decades of measurement theory in educational assessment, specifically Item Response Theory, which has been used to separate examinee ability from item difficulty in test design. It extends recent progress in neural scaling laws—the empirical framework pioneered by works studying how language model performance improves predictably with parameters and compute—by addressing their core practical limitation: the prohibitive cost of evaluating thousands of checkpoints. The paper responds to the tension between the need for accurate scaling predictions and the expense of gathering training data, drawing on insights that latent ability and task characteristics should be disentangled rather than confounded. It also leverages advances in using continuous probability outputs from neural models rather than treating predictions as binary.
enables: This framework enables more efficient prospective scaling analysis, allowing researchers to predict model performance trajectories with significantly fewer evaluations, reducing the computational cost of planning training runs and model development. The factorization approach opens possibilities for transfer learning across benchmarks and model families, where scaling patterns estimated on one set of tasks could inform predictions on new ones. The continuous probability formulation suggests extensions to other domains beyond language models where similar probabilistic outputs exist. The work points toward adaptive evaluation strategies where the most informative checkpoints and questions could be selected strategically rather than exhaustively sampled. It also enables rapid iteration on scaling law estimation itself, facilitating better understanding of how different architectural choices and training procedures affect performance trajectories across diverse evaluation contexts.
